And lawyers have come up with a way to fight that.
And one of the big problems
that we have when the car has been impounded, for example,
for six months, three months being passed,
police cannot prove the charge of failing to stop.
The charge gets withdrawn halfway through.
So the main problem that we have now, who's paying
for the impoundment fee?
Because theoretically police is,
and police have to pay for the impoundment fee.
And they have accepted that on numerous occasions
because the charge is no longer a charge
and the charge has been withdrawn.
So when you pass legislation, you've got
to be careful about the practical element of it
and whether it's going to work in pr, in, in,
in practice or not.
So just repeating myself, we're not here to criticize,
we're just here to make, to help to make it all work.
'cause we know how these things operate on the ground.
Hey, thank you. And very substantive, uh, submission.
I've got, uh, Ms. Nala with a question.
Sure. Hi, uh, hi Sam.
Um, look, thank you for the time
that you all put into these submissions.
It's doesn't go unnoticed.
Um, you were saying that earlier that the sentence, um,
will be punishing other people as well.
Mm-hmm. And, and I just wanna ask you with respect to that,
the sentencing act, as you know, has, um,
purposes of sentencing.
And section seven, subsection one F1 of the purposes is
to deter the offender
or other persons from committing the same
or similar offense.
Mm-hmm. Wouldn't you say that applies here, Samira?
Absolutely. It applies here completely. Okay.
But what I'm saying is that if you have a 19-year-old,
for example, at home and you're asleep, okay,
and they take the car and go
and do whatever stupid, you know, you're the mother,
you know, you've done everything you could have done in your
capacity and you are the one who will end up
with losing the car.
And this is what we see quite often at the moment
that the parents' vehicles that they heavily rely on
for driving just because of the stupidity of their kids,
because the front lobe of the brain is not, uh,
developed properly at the age of 19 or 20.
They're the ones who will end up suffering the consequences.
They can't get the car out of impoundment.
It's very difficult, I can tell you that.
Uh, so they basically have to live with
that financial crisis and not having the use of the vehicle.
My point was that it's an unfair punishment
and a third party at the moment, it's discretionary,
but under the bill it becomes mandatory.
So we are saying that keep that discretion there
if the bill is going to be passed into law,
and uh, don't make it the mandatory element.
Okay, you have a follow
Up, but Samir, by doing this,
the the 19-year-old should learn that.
Look at how much I've made my family and others suffer.
And other people should learn that too.
That's part of the purpose.
That's correct. But then you're looking at,
and you what,
the way you're looking at it, I get your point of view.
Okay? I completely get where you're coming from.
And it frustrates me as well as a lawyer when I have to deal
with young people because they just, you have
to keep telling them, you know,
and it's like, it's, it's a learning process.
But uh, if, if you look at it, if you look at it that way,
you are looking at pa the purpose
of sentencing act is not just punishment, okay?
It's also deterrent and it's also rehabilitation.
So the way the bill is drafted at the moment is mainly
focused on punishment.
Okay? And we do need to look at the other aspect
of the sentencing as well.
So that's where we are coming from to have to keep
that balance and to have that balance.
That's why I said in my submissions, one of the concerns
that we have is that some of these, um, some
of these powers can be exercised as on a first defense.
If you put it as a repeated defending,
that's a different story.
Okay. So, and we do have
that power already under the legislation
and we are saying that it's probably enough.
But, um, I hope that I made my point.
Yeah, you have an excellent point
and thank you very much for your submission this morning.
Thank you very much for the opportunity. Have a good day.
Bye-bye. Bye.
Bye-bye. Now we've got, uh, Danika, uh,
Marshall, uh, by Zoom.
I saw you doing that before. Danika, are you with us?
Uh, yes I am. Nice to hear from you this morning.
Now we've got a few minutes with you.
Um, hopefully you can leave a mine or two for a question.
We've got five minutes.
Um, thank you for the opportunity to speak today.
Um, I'm here to strongly support the passing
of this legislation to tackle antisocial road behavior.
Um, it's a obviously ongoing issue, um,
throughout the country.
Um, it's dangerous, intimidating,
and deeply affects the wellbeing of communities,
including my community also.
Um, I have lived at my current property
for the past three years.
Um, we have a large intersection just down the road.
Um, and this is an ongoing issue every Friday, Saturday, um,
and sometimes during the week as well.
We have really large numbers turn up.
Um, we've experienced obviously the skids and burnouts.
They leave a huge amount of rubbish on the intersection,
destroy road markings.
Um, they pull road signs down.
Um, there's fireworks, alcohol consumption.
Um, they've also completely blocked the intersections at
times so that local residents can't actually get home.
Yeah. Um, we've had countless sleepless nights.
Um, we feel totally unsafe when this is going on.
Um, we've had some even come onto our property.
Um, the police responses are often too late at the moment
and we've got the same offenders returning every single week
because, um,
obviously there's just the current system doesn't give the
police the tools to act, um, fast or effectively enough.
Um, so we need a law that allows for proactive enforcement
and not just reactive.
Um, I believe that police should be able
to immediately arrest offenders and impound vehicles.
Um, I think that hotspots like my intersection,
we need to have, um, cameras, um,
and possibly plate recognition, um, is,
provides both evidence and deterrence.
Um, I think the police need more modern tools
to avoid detection
because often we have large gatherings, um,
and they also, the people are carrying
radios which can tap into the police communications.
So by the time the police get there,
they've already all dispersed, which means that
there's no enforcement.
Um, I think that also the use
of monitoring the different Facebook groups,
because oftentimes the events are pre-planned.
Um, and so you have a large gathering, um,
through social media, um, outlets.
Um, yeah,
I think we need coordinated operations
to stop events in real time
and not just have the cleanup afterwards.
Um, and also a visible police presence.
Alright. Um,
I know I good.
Oh yeah, right. Um, we are just running outta time.
Can, um, I've got people lining up to ask you questions.
Mm-hmm. Can I, um, uh, first one I'm gonna go
with is Mr. Stevenson. Thank
You. Thank you for your submission.
Um, just wanted to understand you,
you mentioned there's actually people coming
onto your property.
I just wanna hear a little bit more about that
'cause it sounds pretty frightening and Yeah,
So acceptable.
We are not, we obviously live not far from the intersection,
so oftentimes it's a, it's a four-way intersection.
So oftentimes there'll be that ma like
that many people turn up there,
that they're actually lined up all the way down each
end of the intersection.
So we've had them outside our house, we've had them just
outside our gate,
and I'm standing there in my house watching,
and obviously the police have not turned up.
I've already called them five
or six times that night, they're not coming.
And I'm like, well, what are we supposed to do?
We, you know, do we take this into our own hands?
What do we do? So it's just got to the point
where we really need, um,
we need the vehicles to be impounded.
We need a precedent set,
and we need this legislation to be a real deterrent
because without this, we have, there's no way
that this is gonna stop.
Okay. Got one last question from Mr. Redford.
Thank you very much. And firstly, thank you for, um,
submitting on this legislation.
It's great to hear from people like yourself
with firsthand lived experience
who live this on some cases on a daily basis.
Yeah. My two questions are, what is the mood
of your wider community?
And I'm meaning your, your next door neighbors as well.
Do they share similar sentiments to you
and are fed up with it?
And secondly, what do you say to the people
who have presented to us with an opposite view to you
that say particularly young men, uh, prior to the age of 25,
aren't fully formed and
therefore shouldn't be held accountable for their actions
because they don't quite know what their actually doing?
Um, well, I think that everyone should be
accountable for their actions.
'cause that's law at the end of the day.
Um, the sentiment of my neighbors in the wider community,
um, there's quite a lot of fear
because there has been times where locals have gone
and, um, you know, obviously tried to
stop these events from happening
and have been threatened and even assaulted.
We've had, we've had locals assaulted.
Um, so there's, it's really difficult
because they know that by calling the police a lot
of the time the police are not turning up.
Um, so it's kind of like, oh, well we just, you know,
you just have to sit tight and deal with it.
Um, but I think that obviously these people
that are attending these events,
they know what they're doing.
Um, if they've got to be accountable
and they've gotta have their cars impounded,
and I don't care if they're their parents' cars
or whatever, the family needs to be aware
that this is an ongoing problem too.
And you know, like I was listening to Samira, the lawyer
previously saying, oh, you know, like,
this needs to be balanced.
But I can tell you right now, if she was living in my house,
she would have a totally different, um,
thought process about it.
Very exactly right. Thank you. Thank you Ms.
Thank you for sharing your personal experiences.
Uh, many of us have, uh, got constituents
who have been in the same situation.
So thank you for coming on
and sharing those personal experiences.
Thank you very much. We,
now we're gonna go back into privacy.
